refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint on the system's operation, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The refined FR focuses on the functional capability—providing visibility into energy efficiency—which is solution-neutral and measurable. Achievement of the energy_per_ton ≤ E_target acceptance criterion is a system-level performance requirement that emerges from correct design of propulsion, hydraulics, and operational control DPs, not from a single FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution can continuously or periodically capture energy_used (kWh or equivalent) and work_output (tons moved or equivalent), compute energy_per_ton, and expose this metric via operator interface, telemetry, or maintenance reporting system. Verify accuracy of measurement against calibrated instrumentation over a representative duty cycle.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure total work output (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, or equivalent productivity metric) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and store the ratio of energy consumed to work output.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide access to energy efficiency metrics to authorized stakeholders (operator, site manager, maintenance).

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline via data aggregation pipeline
DP2: Embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors in the equipment; compute energy_per_ton locally on edge device; stream results to reporting system
DP3: Establish energy metering at equipment fuel/power inlet and tonnage counting at load discharge point; synchronize timestamps and reconcile via post-processing analytics
DP4: Deploy dual measurement streams (energy via utility meter integration; tonnage via independent weighing station); correlate via temporal alignment and statistical regression
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Establish energy metering at equipment fuel/power inlet and tonnage counting at load discharge point; synchronize timestamps and reconcile via post-processing analytics

potential_sources: Temporal synchronization dependency between energy and tonnage measurement subsystems (mitigated by explicit timestamp reconciliation protocol)
Data pipeline coupling to reporting system (mitigated by decoupling via standardized data exchange format and asynchronous aggregation)
Assumption that energy inlet and discharge point are physically accessible and instrumentable (worksite-specific constraint, not design coupling)
coupling_check.notes: DP3 exhibits low functional coupling: energy measurement and tonnage measurement are logically independent; the ratio computation is a pure aggregation function. Temporal synchronization is a coordination concern, not a functional dependency. DP1 and DP3 are equivalent in coupling profile; DP3 is preferred for operational clarity. DP2 introduces edge-device coupling and real-time constraint that is not required by the FR. DP4 introduces statistical regression coupling that adds complexity without necessity.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy metering hardware (fuel flow meter or electrical power meter) is available and can be installed at equipment inlet without operational disruption. Risk: Retrofit constraints on existing equipment may force DP2 (embedded sensors) instead.

Assumption: Tonnage measurement point (load discharge) is accessible and can be instrumented (e.g., weighing scale, load cell). Risk: Mobile or distributed discharge scenarios may require DP4 (distributed weighing stations).

Assumption: Evaluation period is sufficiently long and operationally stable to yield statistically valid energy_per_ton ratio. Risk: High variability in work conditions (terrain, load composition, operator behavior) may require stratification or conditional acceptance criteria.

Assumption: Timestamp synchronization between energy and tonnage streams can be achieved to within acceptable tolerance (e.g., ±5 min). Risk: If synchronization tolerance is tighter, DP2 (co-located sensors) may be necessary.

Assumption: Data aggregation and post-processing can be performed offline without real-time operational feedback. Risk: If real-time energy efficiency feedback is required for operator coaching, DP2 becomes necessary.
Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies permit installation of metering hardware and do not impose constraints that force specific sensor types or locations.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) Required temporal resolution and synchronization tolerance for energy_per_ton computation (e.g., 'computed over shifts' vs. 'computed per load'); (2) Whether real-time feedback to operators is required or offline reporting suffices; (3) Applicability to new vs. retrofit equipment; (4) Acceptable variability in work conditions and whether stratified acceptance criteria (e.g., energy_per_ton by terrain type) are needed. These clarifications will eliminate ambiguity between DP2 and DP3 and reduce risk of design rework.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized energy metering, tonnage counting, timestamp synchronization, and post-processing analytics across all connected vehicles and equipment

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receive fuel/power consumption data and tonnage measurements from Vehicle equipment sensors

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit reconciled energy and tonnage metrics, analytics results, and anomaly reports

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Reliable data transport for sensor streams and analytics outputs across distributed fleet

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE Solution is the fleet-level analytics and monitoring platform. Energy metering, tonnage counting, timestamp synchronization, and post-processing analytics are core NGHE responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry interface leakage for sensor data ingestion and results export.

Poor fit: Vehicle module owns equipment and sensors but lacks fleet-wide analytics capability. Post-processing analytics and cross-vehicle reconciliation cannot be contained at vehicle level. High diffusion: analytics logic would leak to NGHE Solution, creating bidirectional coupling and split responsibility.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is operator interface and remote control. Energy metering and tonnage analytics are not operator concerns and have no logical connection to teleoperation workflows. Severe diffusion: all core logic would reside elsewhere.

Very poor fit: Safety Services handles hazard detection and alerts. Energy/tonnage reconciliation is operational accounting, not safety-critical. Severe diffusion: analytics would leak to NGHE Solution; Safety Services would become a data conduit rather than owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of fleet-level energy metering, tonnage counting, and post-processing analytics. It aggregates telemetry from all vehicles, performs timestamp synchronization, and executes reconciliation logic. Vehicle modules supply raw sensor data via Telemetry interface; NGHE Solution consumes, processes, and publishes results. This assignment respects separation of concerns: vehicles measure, NGHE Solution analyzes.
containment_risks: Sensor calibration and validation logic may need to reside partially in Vehicle if real-time filtering is required before transmission.
Timestamp synchronization assumes reliable clock sources across distributed vehicles; network latency and clock drift could degrade reconciliation accuracy if not carefully managed in DP design.

Post-processing analytics may require iterative refinement; if analytics rules change frequently, consider whether a separate analytics configuration module should be introduced to avoid tight coupling between NGHE Solution and business logic.
If Vehicle modules require immediate feedback on energy/tonnage anomalies for autonomous decision-making, a feedback loop from NGHE Solution to Vehicle may be needed, increasing coupling.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR by specifying a complete measurement and reconciliation approach: energy metering at fuel/power inlet, tonnage counting at load discharge, timestamp synchronization, and post-processing analytics. The DP is logically sound and measurable against the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). No functional gaps identified.

diffusion_risks: Sensor calibration and validation logic: If real-time filtering or anomaly detection is required before data transmission to NGHE Solution, this responsibility may diffuse into Vehicle modules, creating split ownership of data quality assurance.

Timestamp synchronization: Clock drift and network latency across distributed vehicles could degrade reconciliation accuracy. If vehicles require autonomous decision-making based on energy/tonnage anomalies, a feedback loop from NGHE Solution to Vehicle emerges, increasing bidirectional coupling.

Post-processing analytics rules: Frequent business logic changes risk tight coupling between NGHE Solution and analytics configuration. Consider extracting analytics rules into a separate configuration module to reduce diffusion.

Real-time vs. batch processing boundary: DP specifies 'post-processing' but does not clarify whether real-time alerts or batch reconciliation is required. If real-time feedback is needed, analytics logic may diffuse into Vehicle or intermediate edge modules.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with minimal diffusion (0.08). The module boundary is well-defined: Vehicle modules supply raw telemetry (fuel/power, tonnage) via inbound Telemetry interface; NGHE Solution owns aggregation, synchronization, and analytics; results export via outbound Telemetry interface. Separation of concerns is respected. Network Link interface provides reliable transport. No other modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are suitable owners; all alternatives show severe diffusion (0.65–0.85). Recommended ownership assignment is sound.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly measurable from NGHE Solution outputs. Energy consumption data flows from Vehicle sensors → NGHE Solution → reconciled metrics. Tonnage data follows the same path. Timestamp synchronization and post-processing are internal to NGHE Solution and auditable. Evaluation period and target threshold are specified in constraints. No hidden dependencies or unmeasurable requirements.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is the unambiguous owner of the FR realization. Vehicle modules are data suppliers (passive sensors); NGHE Solution is the active analyzer and reporter. Interfaces are explicit: Telemetry (in/out) and Network Link (inout). Ownership boundaries do not overlap. However, clarity could be improved by explicitly documenting: (1) sensor calibration responsibility (Vehicle or NGHE?), (2) real-time vs. batch reconciliation timing, (3) clock synchronization protocol and acceptable drift tolerance, (4) feedback loop requirements (if any) from NGHE to Vehicle for autonomous decisions.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP3 to specify clock synchronization protocol (e.g., NTP, GPS-based, or local reference) and acceptable timestamp drift tolerance to ensure reconciliation accuracy.

Clarify whether post-processing analytics includes real-time anomaly alerts or batch reporting only. If real-time feedback is required, explicitly define the feedback loop from NGHE Solution to Vehicle and its impact on coupling.

Document sensor calibration and validation responsibility: specify whether Vehicle modules perform pre-transmission filtering or NGHE Solution handles all validation post-ingestion. This prevents diffusion of data quality assurance logic.

Consider extracting analytics rules (thresholds, reconciliation algorithms, anomaly definitions) into a separate configuration module or policy layer to decouple NGHE Solution from frequent business logic changes.

Add acceptance criterion detail: specify the evaluation period duration, frequency of reconciliation (daily, weekly, per-shift), and tolerance for timestamp synchronization errors (e.g., ±5 seconds) to make verification unambiguous.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and the NGHE Solution module boundary is well-justified (0.92 containment, minimal diffusion). However, the recommended_revisions identify critical ambiguities in DP3 that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-level decomposition. Specifically: (1) clock synchronization protocol and drift tolerance are unspecified, creating reconciliation accuracy risk; (2) real-time vs. batch processing boundary is unclear, risking diffusion of analytics logic into Vehicle modules; (3) sensor calibration responsibility is ambiguous, potentially splitting data quality ownership; (4) feedback loop requirements from NGHE Solution to Vehicle are not documented. Given high safety criticality, these ambiguities must be resolved at the current level to prevent downstream architectural defects and ensure verification feasibility. Proceeding to sub-level decomposition without clarification would propagate uncertainty into lower-level FRs and risk rework.

revise.why: DP3 is logically sound but insufficiently detailed for safe decomposition. Revisions must address: (1) explicit clock synchronization protocol (NTP, GPS-based, or local reference) with acceptable drift tolerance (e.g., ±5 seconds); (2) clear boundary between real-time anomaly detection and batch reconciliation, with explicit definition of any feedback loops from NGHE Solution to Vehicle; (3) documented responsibility for sensor calibration and pre-transmission validation (Vehicle or NGHE Solution); (4) extraction of analytics rules into a separate configuration/policy module to reduce coupling and enable independent business logic updates; (5) detailed acceptance criterion specifying evaluation period duration, reconciliation frequency (daily/weekly/per-shift), and timestamp synchronization error tolerance. These revisions transform DP3 from a high-level concept into an implementable design specification suitable for sub-level decomposition.

assumptions: Clock synchronization across distributed vehicles is achievable within acceptable drift tolerance (assumed ±5 seconds, to be confirmed).
Sensor calibration and validation can be cleanly assigned to either Vehicle modules (pre-transmission) or NGHE Solution (post-ingestion) without split ownership (to be clarified).

Post-processing analytics does not require real-time feedback loops to Vehicle modules for autonomous decision-making (to be confirmed; if real-time feedback is required, coupling and safety implications must be analyzed).

Analytics rules (thresholds, reconciliation algorithms, anomaly definitions) can be externalized into a configuration module without compromising NGHE Solution's core ownership (to be validated during detailed design).

Evaluation period, reconciliation frequency, and timestamp tolerance are within the scope of constraints and can be specified without requiring additional system capabilities (to be confirmed with stakeholders).
open_risks: Clock drift and network latency across distributed vehicles could degrade reconciliation accuracy below acceptable thresholds if synchronization protocol is not rigorously specified and validated. High safety criticality amplifies this risk.

Ambiguity in real-time vs. batch processing boundary may lead to diffusion of analytics logic into Vehicle modules or intermediate edge components, violating separation of concerns and increasing bidirectional coupling.

Sensor calibration responsibility split between Vehicle and NGHE Solution could result in duplicated validation logic or gaps in data quality assurance, compromising energy_per_ton metric reliability.

Frequent business logic changes to analytics rules (thresholds, anomaly definitions) risk tight coupling between NGHE Solution and configuration, increasing maintenance burden and change propagation risk.

Feedback loop requirements from NGHE Solution to Vehicle (if needed for autonomous decisions) are not documented, creating potential for uncontrolled coupling and safety-critical interaction patterns.
evidence_needed: Specification of clock synchronization protocol (NTP, GPS-based, local reference, or hybrid) with measured drift tolerance and validation method.

Clarification of real-time vs. batch processing boundary: define whether NGHE Solution must generate real-time anomaly alerts or batch reconciliation reports only; if real-time feedback is required, document feedback loop design and safety implications.

Documented responsibility matrix for sensor calibration and validation: specify whether Vehicle modules perform pre-transmission filtering/validation or NGHE Solution handles all validation post-ingestion; include acceptance criteria for sensor accuracy and calibration frequency.

Design of configuration/policy module for analytics rules: specify how thresholds, reconciliation algorithms, and anomaly definitions are externalized, versioned, and deployed without requiring NGHE Solution code changes.

Detailed acceptance criterion specification: evaluation period duration (e.g., 30 days), reconciliation frequency (daily/weekly/per-shift), timestamp synchronization error tolerance (e.g., ±5 seconds), and method for verifying energy_per_ton ≤ E_target against ground truth.
Safety analysis of clock synchronization failures, sensor calibration drift, and feedback loop latency to confirm that high safety criticality is adequately addressed by the revised DP3.
